1: dio aphentes ton tēs archēs tou Christou logon epi tēn teleiotēta pherōmetha — "therefore let us leave the elementary doctrine of Christ and go on to maturity" (tēs archēs tou Christou logon: the word of the beginning of Christ — the foundational message; teleiotēta: maturity, completeness — the teleio- root again): aphentes (having left — aorist participle: a completed departure) and pherōmetha (let us be carried, be swept along toward — the passive suggests the Spirit's movement): the author does not call the foundation worthless but insists on building on it rather than always re-laying it. The six foundational items (repentance, faith, baptisms, hand-laying, resurrection, eternal judgment) represent Jewish catechesis that the community has mastered.
4: adynaton gar tous hapax phōtisthentas, geusamenous te tēs dōreas tēs epouraniou kai metochous genēthentas pneumatos hagiou kai kalon geusamenous theou rhēma dynameis te mellontos aiōnos, kai parapersontas, palin anakainizein eis metanoian — "for it is impossible, in the case of those who have once been enlightened, who have tasted the heavenly gift, and have shared in the Holy Spirit, and have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the age to come, and then have fallen away, to restore them again to repentance" (adynaton: impossible — the strong impossibility claim): the participles describe genuine spiritual experience: phōtisthentas (enlightened — a NT term for baptism), geusamenous (having tasted — genuine experiential contact, not merely intellectual acquaintance), metochous genēthentas pneumatos hagiou (having become sharers in the Holy Spirit). Parapersontas (having fallen alongside, having fallen away — the para-compound: falling beside the path): the cause of the impossibility (v6): "since they are crucifying once more the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt."
13: tō gar Abraam epangeilamenos ho theos, epei kat' oudenos eichen meizōnos omosai, ōmosen kath' heautou — "for when God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself" (epei kat' oudenos eichen meizōnos omosai: since by no one greater he had to swear — the divine self-swearing is necessitated by the absence of a greater authority): human oaths invoke a greater authority to confirm what is said; God, having no greater authority, swears by his own name. The logic establishes the absolute certainty of the divine promise.
17: en hō perissoteron boulomenos ho theos epideixai tois klēronōmois tēs epangelias to ametatheton tēs boulēs autou emesiteusen horkō — "so when God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose, he guaranteed it with an oath" (boulomenos: desiring, wishing — deliberate divine will; ametatheton tēs boulēs autou: the unalterableness/immutability of his purpose — ametathetos: unable to be moved from, irrevocable): the divine oath is not required for God's truthfulness but is given for the benefit of the heirs — the double guarantee (promise + oath) gives maximum assurance to those who need confidence.
19: hēn hōs ankyran echomen tēs psychēs asphalē te kai bebaian kai eisercomenēn eis to esōteron tou katapetasmatos — "we have this as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul, a hope that enters into the inner place behind the curtain" (ankyran tēs psychēs: anchor of the soul — the nautical metaphor of anchor applied to eschatological hope: ancient anchors were dropped into a safe harbor to hold the ship against current and wind; hope in the heavenly sanctuary holds the soul against the currents of the present age): asphalē te kai bebaian (sure and steadfast) — two terms of reliability. Eisercomenēn eis to esōteron tou katapetasmatos (entering into the inner sanctuary behind the curtain) — hope penetrates the sanctuary barrier where Jesus the forerunner has already entered.
2: baptismōn didachēs, epitheseōs te cheirōn, anastaseōs te nekrōn kai kritatos aiōniou — "the teaching about baptisms, the laying on of hands, the resurrection of the dead, and eternal judgment" (baptismōn: the plural is notable — Jewish purification rites used multiple ritual washings, and early Christian instruction had to distinguish Christian baptism from Jewish mikveh, John's baptism, and proselyte baptism): cheirōn epithesis (laying on of hands — a formal gesture of commissioning, blessing, or Spirit-conferral used in both OT and NT); anastaseōs nekrōn and kritatos aiōniou (resurrection of the dead and eternal judgment) are paired eschatological realities — the physical resurrection leads directly to the final assize. The six items form a catechetical curriculum.
3: kai touto poiēsomen, eanper epitrepē ho theos — "and God permitting, we will do so" (eanper epitrepē ho theos: if indeed God permits — eanper: third-class condition expressing contingency; epitrepē: subjunctive of epitrepō: to allow, to turn over to): the brief aside acknowledges that the theological progress the author proposes is itself subject to divine permission. The conditional is not a theological hedge about the author's willingness but a genuine acknowledgment that advancing toward maturity requires divine enablement — the same God who commands maturity must also grant the capacity to move toward it.
5: kai kalon geusamenous theou rhēma dynameis te mellontos aiōnos — "who have tasted the goodness of the word of God and the powers of the coming age" (geusamenous: having tasted — aorist participle; geuomai means genuine experiential contact, not mere surface acquaintance; cf. Ps 34:8 "taste and see that the LORD is good"): kalon rhēma (the goodness/quality of the word): the adjective kalon here is experiential — the word of God proves itself good to those who engage it. Dynameis mellontos aiōnos (powers of the coming age): the miraculous acts performed in the community are advance installments of the age-to-come's power — already operative before the age itself arrives fully.
6: kai parapersontas, palin anakainizein eis metanoian, anastaurountas heautois ton huion tou theou kai paradeigmatizontas — "and who have then fallen away — to restore them again to repentance, since they are crucifying once more the Son of God to their own harm and holding him up to contempt" (parapersontas: falling alongside/past — NT hapax from parapipto: to fall away, to slip past; the para-prefix suggests falling beside the path): the reason repentance is impossible is stated in the two participial clauses: anastaurountas (crucifying again — ana-: again, anew) and paradeigmatizontas (publicly exposing to shame — from paradeigma: an example displayed publicly). To pursue "restoration" to repentance would be to treat the first repentance as insufficient, implying Christ's sacrifice needs repeating.
7: gē gar hē piousa ton ep' autēs erchomenon pollakis hueton, kai tiktousa botanēn eutheton ekeinois di' hous kai geōrgeitai, metalambanei eulogias apo tou theou — "for land that drinks in the rain often falling on it and that produces a crop useful to those for whom it is farmed receives the blessing of God" (piousa: having drunk — aorist participle; the land's reception of rain is a completed act leading to production; tiktousa botanēn eutheton: producing fit/useful vegetation — euthetos: suited to purpose, well-fitted): the agricultural metaphor: the same rain (the same word, the same gifts of the Spirit) falls on both land types; the difference is not in the rain but in the land's response. The rain does not discriminate.
8: ekpherouса de akanthas kai tribolous adokimos kai kataras engys, hēs to telos eis kausin — "but land that produces thorns and thistles is worthless and in danger of being cursed. In the end it will be burned" (adokimos: failing the test, rejected, disqualified — from a-privative + dokimazō: to test and approve): land that fails to produce the expected crop fails the test of its purpose; kataras engys (near a curse — not yet cursed, but on the verge): the "nearness" language is merciful — it is not certain doom but an imminent danger. To telos eis kausin (the end toward burning): the burning is the agricultural clearing that allows a new planting cycle — judgment with the possibility of renewal, not just annihilation.
9: pepeismetha de peri hymōn, agapētoi, ta kreissona kai echomena sōtērias, ei kai houtōs laloumen — "even though we speak like this, dear friends, we are convinced of better things in your case — the things that belong to salvation" (pepeismetha: we are persuaded — perfect passive of peithō: the author stands in a state of settled persuasion about his community; the perfect tense conveys an abiding conviction): agapētoi (beloved — a direct address that shifts the emotional register after the severe warning): the turn signals that the warning is not a verdict but a pastoral alert issued to those the author expects to remain faithful. Echomena sōtērias (holding to/touching salvation — echomai: to cling to, to hold onto): the things that accompany salvation.
10: ou gar adikos ho theos epilathesthai tou ergou hymōn kai tēs agapēs hēs enedeixasthe eis to onoma autou, diakonēsantes tois hagiois kai diakonountes — "for God is not unjust so as to overlook your work and the love that you have shown for his name in serving the saints, as you still do" (ou gar adikos ho theos: for God is not unjust — the divine justice is invoked as the ground of certainty; the community's persevering service cannot go unrewarded because that would be unjust): epilathesthai (to forget — aorist infinitive of epilanthanomai: the divine forgetting is what is ruled out): diakonēsantes... kai diakonountes (having served... and still serving — the aorist + present combination: past service complete and continuing present service).
11: epithumomen de hekaston hymōn tēn autēn endeiknysthai spoudēn pros tēn plērophorian tēs elpidos achri telous — "we want each of you to show this same diligence to the very end, so as to make your hope sure" (epithumomen: we desire/long for — not merely "we want" as a casual preference but earnest longing [epithumia = passionate desire]): plērophorian tēs elpidos (full assurance of hope — plērophoria: full carrying through, complete persuasion, assurance that fills): hope is not uncertain wishing but confident expectation that must be maintained with the same diligence that produced past service. Achri telous (until the end): the endpoint is eschatological — the goal is not just present faithfulness but sustained faith to the consummation.
12: hina mē nōthroi genēsthe, mimētai de tōn dia pisteōs kai makrothymias klēronountōn tas epangelias — "so that you will not be sluggish, but will imitate those who through faith and patient endurance inherit what has been promised" (nōthroi: sluggish, dull, slow — the same word used in 5:11 for dullness of hearing; here it describes the spiritual torpor that causes one to stop before the finish line): mimētai (imitators — from mimeomai: to imitate, to model oneself on): dia pisteōs kai makrothymias (through faith and long-suffering patience — makrothymia: long-tempered endurance, the opposite of short-tempered capitulation under pressure): faith and makrothymia are the two instruments by which the promised inheritance is received.
14: legōn, Ei mēn eulogōn eulogēsō se kai plēthynōn plēthynō se — "saying: I will surely bless you and give you many descendants" (Gen 22:17 LXX): eulogōn eulogēsō and plēthynōn plēthynō — the doubled infinitive-verb construction translates the Hebrew infinitive absolute (bāraḵ ʾăbāreḵeḵā): in the LXX, this idiom intensifies the verb to express absolute certainty and completeness. The translation "I will surely bless / I will surely multiply" captures this but flattens the rhetorical force of the reduplication that signals an oath-level commitment.
15: kai houtōs makrothymēsas epetachen tēs epangelias — "and so after waiting patiently, Abraham received what was promised" (makrothymēsas: having endured patiently — aorist participle of makrothymeō: the same makrothymia root of v12): Abraham exemplifies the faith-and-patience combination the community is called to imitate. Epetachen tēs epangelias (received, attained the promise — epetynchanō: to hit the mark, to obtain, to attain): the verb implies directed movement toward a goal that is successfully reached.
16: anthrōpoi gar kata tou meizonos omnuousin, kai pasēs autois antilogias peras eis bebaiosin ho horkos — "for people swear by someone greater than themselves, and the oath ends all dispute by confirming what is said" (kata tou meizonos omnuousin: they swear by the greater — the invocation of a superior authority [God, or the emperor, or the gods] to back the claim): antilogias peras (end of contradiction/dispute — antilogia: counter-speaking, contradiction): the oath's social function is to terminate debate by placing a claim under divine sanction. Eis bebaiosin (for confirmation — bebaios: legally reliable, secure): the oath makes the statement legally secure.
18: hina dia duo pragma ametatheton, en hois adynaton pseudesthai theon, ischyran paraklēsin echōmen hoi kataphygontes kratēsai tēs prokeimenēs elpidos — "so that by two unchangeable things, in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us" (adynaton pseudesthai theon: it is impossible for God to lie — the same adynaton of 6:4; what is impossible for apostates to recover is paralleled by what is impossible for God to do, anchoring the impossibility on God's character): kataphygontes (having fled to refuge — the image of the one seeking sanctuary, the city of refuge): the community has already fled to God's promise; they need encouragement to remain, not to flee again.
20: hopou prodromos hyper hēmōn eisēlthen Iēsous, kata tēn taxin Melchisedek archiereus genomenos eis ton aiōna — "where our forerunner, Jesus, has entered on our behalf. He has become a high priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (prodromos: forerunner, one who runs ahead to prepare the way — a NT hapax; the term was used for advance scouting parties and for the first fruits of the harvest): Jesus is not merely the door to the sanctuary but the first to enter it, paving the way for others to follow the same path. Eis ton aiōna (forever) — the permanence of the Melchizedekian priesthood is the guarantee that the anchor holds permanently.
